summary: I grew up in California, specifically in the Bay Area near Silicon Valley, in a town called Los Altos. I left California in 1980 but returned frequently to visit my mother until she passed in 2017. California was a beautiful place with lots of nature, like the Sierras and Santa Cruz Beach, but I also witnessed the environmental changes and urban development that concerned me deeply.

I moved to Kentucky in 1989 when I took a job at Eastern Kentucky University. Although I wasn't a native Kentuckian, I eventually assimilated into the culture here, which was quite different from California. My husband worked at Kentucky State University, and we raised our two sons here. Over time, I became involved in local community events and organizations, especially after retiring in 2016.

I started working at Eastern Kentucky University in 1989 in the psychology department and taught there for about 27 years until I retired in 2016. It was a big change from California, and it took me some time to understand the campus culture and student expectations. EKU became my professional home and a place where I developed my research interests, especially in cognitive psychology and environmental decision-making.

My husband got a job at Kentucky State University before I moved to Kentucky, which is why I followed to the state. His position there was a key factor in our decision to settle in Kentucky. While I worked at EKU, his role at Kentucky State helped anchor our family life in the area.

One of our sons lives in Houston, where he works as a high school teacher. Although I don't know all the details of his work, I'm proud that he has established his own career there. Houston represents part of our family's spread across the country.

Our other son lives in San Francisco and works in the tech industry, though I mostly just know he's a 'techie.' We visit California regularly, so I get to see him and observe the changes in the Bay Area and Silicon Valley. San Francisco is part of our family's connection back to California.

I became involved with Kentuckians for the Commonwealth through a friend, Steve Wilkins, around 2016 or 2017. Under Lisa Abbott's leadership, KFTC organized the On the Table event focused on Kentucky's energy future, especially in response to the Obama administration's Clean Power Plan. I joined their New Energy and Transition Committee and have been active in their efforts for a just energy transition in Kentucky ever since.

The On the Table event, organized by Kentuckians for the Commonwealth around 2016 or 2017, was a statewide gathering to discuss Kentucky's energy future. It was partly a grassroots response to Kentucky's refusal to implement the Obama administration's Clean Power Plan. The event gathered input from many people and produced a comprehensive document on just transition strategies, which impressed me and led to my involvement in the related committee.

Lisa Abbott was the director of the New Energy and Transition Team Committee at Kentuckians for the Commonwealth when they organized the On the Table event. She played a key role in leading efforts to engage Kentuckians on energy issues and just transition strategies. I admired her leadership and the work she and her team did to create meaningful community dialogue.

The Obama administration introduced the Clean Power Plan, which aimed to reduce carbon emissions by requiring states to develop their own implementation strategies. Kentucky, however, refused to participate, which led local groups like Kentuckians for the Commonwealth to organize their own initiatives such as the On the Table event. This federal policy indirectly inspired grassroots energy discussions in Kentucky that I became involved with.

I grew up in the Bay Area, near what is now Silicon Valley, in a town called Los Altos. Over the years, I've witnessed tremendous changes there, especially with the growth of tech companies like Google. Although I left California in 1980, I visited the Bay Area regularly until my mother passed in 2017, observing how much the region transformed.

Silicon Valley was near where I grew up in Los Altos, California. When I was young, it was very different from the tech hub it is today. Over the years, I saw it evolve dramatically, especially during visits back to California, which gave me a unique perspective on environmental and social changes tied to technological growth.

I was raised in Los Altos, a town near Silicon Valley in California. It was a suburban area surrounded by orchards when I was young, but over time, much of that land was developed into housing and tech campuses. My experiences growing up there shaped my awareness of environmental issues and urban expansion.

Growing up in California, my family often visited Santa Cruz Beach, which was a beautiful natural spot close enough for us to enjoy regularly. Those visits helped foster my appreciation for nature and the environment. It was part of the rich natural landscape of California that I loved and wanted to protect.

I remember the first Earth Day when I was in high school, which was a significant event that sparked my early environmental awareness. There was even a student conference where the opening statement was that we would all be dead by 1980, reflecting the urgency felt at the time. This event, along with talks by figures like Paul Ehrlich, deeply influenced my environmental consciousness.

Paul Ehrlich gave a talk at my high school during the early environmental movement, around the time of the first Earth Day. His presence and message contributed to the sense of urgency about environmental issues that many of us felt as students. This experience was part of what shaped my lifelong interest in environmental concerns.

Stanford University was near where I grew up in California, and I was aware of the campus and its influence. During my high school years, many environmental events and speakers, including Paul Ehrlich, were connected to Stanford. The university's proximity contributed to the environmental culture I was exposed to growing up.

I attended Oregon State University for my first two years of college before transferring to UC Berkeley. This was part of my early higher education journey before settling into my major and career path. Oregon State was an important step in my academic development.

I transferred to UC Berkeley after two years at Oregon State and completed my undergraduate degree there. While I was initially frustrated with the education style, which seemed focused on memorization, Berkeley exposed me to ideas like those of Jean Piaget and sparked my interest in psychology. It was a formative place for my academic and personal growth.

I started a master's program at San Francisco State University before moving on to a PhD program. This was an important step in my graduate education and helped me refine my focus in psychology. San Francisco State was part of my academic journey on the West Coast before relocating to Kentucky.

I have a colleague at Carnegie Mellon University who works with cognitive scientists, and we have discussed overlaps between rhetoric and cognitive psychology. Although I did not study there, my professional network includes people from Carnegie Mellon, which has influenced some of my thinking and collaborations.

Jean Piaget was a developmental psychologist whose work on cognitive development in children inspired me to pursue developmental psychology. I first read about Piaget as an undergraduate and was fascinated by his theories. His influence guided my early academic interests before I specialized further in cognitive psychology.
